Systems Programming

Understanding Rust's Ownership Model: A Visual Guide

Marcus Chen February 28, 2024 12 min read

Rust's ownership system is its most unique feature. It enables memory safety without garbage collection. But it takes time to learn. This guide breaks it down visually.

The Three Rules

Rust's ownership system follows three simple rules:

  1. Each value in Rust has an owner
  2. There can only be one owner at a time
  3. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped

Moves

When you assign a value to another variable, ownership moves:

fn main() {
    let s1 = String::from("hello");
    let s2 = s1;  // s1 is moved to s2
    
    // println!("{}", s1);  // ERROR: s1 is no longer valid
    println!("{}", s2);     // OK: s2 owns the string
}

This prevents double-free errors. The compiler tracks ownership at compile time, so there's zero runtime cost.

References and Borrowing

Instead of transferring ownership, you can lend a reference:

fn calculate_length(s: &String) -> usize {
    s.len()  // s is a reference, we don't own it
}

fn main() {
    let s = String::from("hello");
    let len = calculate_length(&s);  // borrow s
    println!("{} has length {}", s, len);  // s is still valid!
}

References are immutable by default. You can have multiple immutable references OR one mutable reference, but not both simultaneously.

Lifetimes

Lifetimes ensure references don't outlive the data they point to. The compiler usually infers them, but sometimes you need to be explicit:

fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str {
    if x.len() > y.len() { x } else { y }
}

The 'a annotation says: "the returned reference lives at least as long as both inputs."

Real-World Example

Here's how ownership prevents common bugs in practice:

fn process_data(data: Vec) -> i32 {
    data.iter().sum()  // data is dropped after this
}

fn main() {
    let numbers = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
    let total = process_data(numbers);
    // numbers is gone - can't accidentally use stale data
    println!("Sum: {}", total);
}

Key Takeaways

  • Ownership prevents memory leaks and use-after-free bugs
  • Borrowing allows reading data without taking ownership
  • Lifetimes ensure references are always valid
  • All checks happen at compile time - zero runtime overhead
rust systems-programming memory-safety
